title: Oil and Petroleum Products | Council on Foreign Relations
gist: CFR's Oil and Petroleum Products archive page shows the site's coverage converging on Gulf/Hormuz supply risk from the Iran war, alongside parallel storylines on Venezuela's opaque oil-revenue recovery, UAE's OPEC exit, sanctions-evasion via shadow fleets, and Guyana's emergence as a new supply source.

themes.theme: Strait of Hormuz as the Central Supply Risk

insights.claim: CFR frames the Strait of Hormuz as a persistent U.S.-Iran maritime flash point as of mid-July 2026, with continued risk to Gulf oil and gas flows.
insights.speaker: Council on Foreign Relations
insights.so_what: Confirms Hormuz remains the dominant near-term tail risk for global oil supply and freight rates.
insights.quote: The Strait of Hormuz: A U.S.-Iran Maritime Flash Point

insights.claim: The Iran war turned Asia's already-fragile energy dependence into an emergency, per CFR's June 23, 2026 coverage.
insights.speaker: Council on Foreign Relations
insights.so_what: Reinforces Asia as the region most exposed to a Gulf supply disruption, a channel to watch for regional inflation and import-bill stress.
insights.locator: Expert Take, June 23, 2026, by Joshua Kurlantzick

themes.theme: Venezuela's Oil Revenue and Output Questions

insights.claim: CFR raises questions about where the money has gone after the U.S. took over Venezuela's oil industry, implying a lack of transparency in revenue flows.
insights.speaker: Council on Foreign Relations
insights.so_what: A governance/transparency flag worth monitoring for anyone evaluating Venezuela-linked oil assets or U.S. policy toward the sector.
insights.quote: The U.S. Took Over Venezuela’s Oil Industry. Where Has All the Money Gone?

insights.claim: CFR analysis concludes that increasing Venezuela's oil output will take several years and billions of dollars of investment, tempering expectations of a quick supply boost.
insights.speaker: Council on Foreign Relations
insights.so_what: Investors should not price in near-term Venezuelan supply relief to global oil markets; the ramp-up timeline is structurally long.

insights.claim: CFR covers the UAE's exit from OPEC and its implications for the cartel's future cohesion.
insights.speaker: Council on Foreign Relations
insights.so_what: A major Gulf producer leaving OPEC weakens the cartel's ability to coordinate output and defend price floors.
insights.quote: Why the UAE Walked Out on OPEC—and What It Means for the Cartel

insights.claim: CFR held a dedicated meeting on 'shadow fleets' as the future frontier of U.S. oil sanctions enforcement, indicating sanctioned crude continues to move via opaque tanker networks.
insights.speaker: Council on Foreign Relations
insights.so_what: Sanctions-evasion shipping capacity remains a live enforcement gap affecting the real-world effectiveness of oil sanctions on Iran and Russia.

insights.claim: CFR examines whether Trump's gamble of easing oil sanctions on both Iran and Russia will pay off.
insights.speaker: Council on Foreign Relations
insights.so_what: Sanctions-relief policy toward two major sanctioned oil exporters simultaneously is a distinct swing factor for global crude supply.

themes.theme: New Supply Sources and Market Structure

insights.claim: CFR frames Guyana's oil boom as a factor set to reshape global energy security, signaling a growing non-OPEC, non-Middle East supply source.
insights.speaker: Council on Foreign Relations
insights.so_what: Guyana is an emerging structural diversification story for oil supply away from Gulf chokepoint risk.
insights.quote: How Guyana’s Oil Boom Will Reshape Energy Security
insights.locator: February 11, 2026, by Roxanna Vigil and Diana Roy

insights.claim: CFR's coverage includes a piece titled 'Petrodollars: Myth and Reality,' suggesting the conventional petrodollar-recycling narrative may be overstated relative to actual capital flows.
insights.speaker: Council on Foreign Relations
insights.so_what: Flags the petrodollar thesis as a topic where CFR's own author sees a gap between popular narrative and underlying reality — worth reading closely for macro investors who rely on the petrodollar framework.

insights.claim: CFR maintains a 'Global Energy Tracker' tool monitoring global energy flows amid the disruptions of the Iran war and related sanctions activity.
insights.speaker: Council on Foreign Relations
insights.so_what: Indicates a live, ongoing CFR data resource for tracking real-time shifts in global oil and gas flows.
insights.locator: Global Energy Tracker, February 23, 2026, by Benn Steil and Yuma Schuster
